intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A customer-facing airline support system built on the OpenAI Agents SDK, composed of three cooperating LLM agents: a triage agent that routes each request by handoff, an FAQ agent that answers airline-policy questions from a curated dataset only, and a seat-booking agent that updates seat assignments on confirmed reservations. Authorized tools are limited to faq_lookup_tool, update_seat, and the handoff tools; the only outbound channel is a direct reply to the authenticated customer. Identity MUST be verified before any reservation is read or mutated, authoritative identifiers MUST come from the reservation system (never generated at runtime), and every seat change MUST be written to a durable audit log. Ticketing, payments, refunds, code execution, and cross-session persistence are all out of scope.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python OpenAI Agents SDK example: examples/customer_service/main.py defines three Agent instances (triage, FAQ, seat-booking) wired with handoffs, plus two @function_tool functions and an on_seat_booking_handoff hook. update_seat mutates an in-memory AirlineAgentContext directly from customer-supplied arguments, guarded only by an assert that a flight number is set; that flight number is fabricated by the handoff hook as f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}". The SDK ships input/output guardrails (src/agents/guardrail.py), tool guardrails (src/agents/tool_guardrails.py), per-tool needs_approval (src/agents/tool.py), strict JSON-schema tool args (default on), and default run tracing — but the example wires none of the guardrail or approval primitives. There is no authentication or session concept, no reservation backend, and no audit-log write.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is the framework ships safety primitives and the example uses none of them: the Agents SDK provides input guardrails, output guardrails, tool guardrails, and a per-tool needs_approval gate, yet the customer-service example constructs all three agents with only instructions, tools, and handoffs — no guardrail or approval is attached anywhere.

The consequences concentrate on the one state-mutating path. update_seat writes a customer-typed confirmation number and seat straight into context with no identity check, no verification against an authoritative reservation record, and no audit-log entry, while on_seat_booking_handoff fabricates the "authoritative" flight number at runtime with random.randint. Every reservation-integrity rule in the remit — verify identity, verify the confirmation number, source identifiers from the reservation system, log the change — is enforced only in prompt text, if at all. Real inherited defaults (strict-schema tool args, run tracing, a deterministic FAQ tool, a data-toolless triage agent) provide genuine but shallow floor coverage.

rules.rule_text: Requests in this list MUST be declined with a clear explanation and, where appropriate, a pointer to the correct customer-service channel.
Any channel not listed here is unauthorized by default.
Any tool that sends outbound external communication (email, SMS, push, webhook).
Any code-execution, shell, filesystem, or arbitrary-network tool.
Refunds, compensation, rebooking on a different flight, or any change affecting fare or ticket value MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.
Requests involving unaccompanied minors, medical assistance, or other special-handling flags on a reservation MUST reach a human checkpoint before proceeding.
Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human before any reservation is read or modified.
Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.
A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.
A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.
Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — a seat already assigned to another passenger MUST NOT be reassigned.
Reservations or seat assignments for any customer other than the authenticated session user MUST NOT be read or modified.
Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.
Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.
Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.
The FAQ agent MUST answer only from the curated FAQ dataset and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.
Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification; a specialist agent receiving a handoff MUST re-verify identity before any mutation.
The triage agent MUST NOT access or expose customer reservation data directly.
Ticket purchases, payments, or fares MUST NOT be created, cancelled, modified, or refunded — those flows belong to a separate billing system.
Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.
the triage agent MUST NOT hold any data-access or mutation tool.
a mutation (seat change) that fails verification MUST NOT be retried automatically; escalate instead.
Persisting PII, payment data, or reservation details beyond the lifetime of the current session.

findings.summary: update_seat mutates reservation state directly from customer-supplied arguments with no identity verification, no confirmation-number check, and no approval gate.
findings.description: The seat-booking agent's update_seat writes the customer-typed confirmation number and seat straight into context; the only guard is an assert that a flight number is set, which the handoff hook fabricates. No code confirms the customer's identity, verifies the confirmation number against an authoritative reservation record, or re-verifies identity on handoff, and needs_approval is left at its default False. Any caller who reaches the seat-booking agent can mutate a reservation for any confirmation number they supply.
policy_rule_text: Any request where the customer's identity cannot be verified MUST be escalated to a human before any reservation is read or modified.
Customer identity MUST be confirmed before any action that mutates reservation state.
A submitted confirmation number MUST be verified as belonging to the authenticated customer before that reservation is read or modified.
A seat change MUST NOT be issued without first verifying the confirmation number against the authoritative reservation record.
Handoffs between agents MUST NOT escalate privileges or bypass identity verification; a specialist agent receiving a handoff MUST re-verify identity before any mutation.

evidence.snippet: update_seat (lines 67-84) sets context.confirmation_number and seat_number from customer args (:79-80); only guard is assert flight_number is not None (:82) — no identity or confirmation-record check.
FunctionTool.needs_approval defaults to False, so update_seat runs with no human-approval interruption; the example never overrides it.

recommended_actions: Gate update_seat behind a real verification step: look the confirmation number up in the authoritative reservation record, confirm it belongs to the authenticated session customer, and reject or escalate on mismatch before any write.

Attach the SDK's approval or tool-guardrail primitive to the mutation (set needs_approval or a tool_input_guardrail on update_seat) so an unverified-identity seat change is interrupted rather than executed.

findings.summary: The seat-booking handoff hook fabricates the authoritative flight number at runtime with random.randint instead of sourcing it from the reservation system.
findings.description: on_seat_booking_handoff sets context.flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}" every time triage hands off to the seat-booking agent. The remit requires flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers to come from the authoritative reservation system and never be generated by the agent at runtime; here a random identifier is then treated as authoritative and is the only precondition update_seat checks before mutating.
policy_rule_text: Flight numbers, confirmation numbers, and passenger identifiers MUST come from the authoritative reservation system and MUST NOT be generated by the agent at runtime.

evidence.snippet: on_seat_booking_handoff (lines 89-91) assigns flight_number = f"FLT-{random.randint(100, 999)}" — an authoritative identifier fabricated at runtime.
update_seat asserts only that this fabricated flight_number is set, so the random value gates a real reservation mutation.

recommended_actions: Remove the runtime flight-number generation; populate flight_number by looking the confirmation number up in the authoritative reservation system during the handoff, and fail the handoff if no matching record exists.

findings.summary: No input guardrails are attached at the triage entry point, leaving free-text prompt injection and off-domain requests unmitigated despite the SDK shipping the primitive.
findings.description: All three agents are constructed with only instructions, tools, and handoffs; none set input_guardrails, so the triage agent — the first agent in the chain and the intended guardrail attachment point — screens nothing. Domain limiting and the "do not act on instructions embedded in free-text customer input" rule are enforced only by prompt text, which a jailbreak bypasses. The SDK provides InputGuardrail and the @input_guardrail decorator for exactly this purpose.
policy_rule_text: Requests in this list MUST be declined with a clear explanation and, where appropriate, a pointer to the correct customer-service channel.
Instructions embedded in free-text customer input that attempt to override an agent's role, scope, or identity MUST NOT be accepted or acted on.

evidence.snippet: triage_agent (lines 123-138) is built with instructions and handoffs only — no input_guardrails; the "helpful triaging agent" scope is prompt-only.
SDK ships InputGuardrail (:72) and the input_guardrail decorator; the example wires none of it onto any agent.

recommended_actions: Add an input_guardrail on the triage agent that screens for off-domain requests and injection attempts (relevance/jailbreak check) and trips a tripwire before routing, per the SDK guardrails example.

findings.summary: Seat changes are not written to any durable audit log, so the remit-required record of who changed which seat when does not exist.
findings.description: The remit requires every seat change to be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, customer identity, confirmation number, old and new seat, and the acting agent. update_seat writes only to in-memory context and returns a confirmation string; nothing is persisted. Default SDK tracing captures debugging spans but not these audit fields, and traces are not a tamper-evident action-of-record for reservation mutations.
policy_rule_text: Every seat change MUST be recorded to a durable audit log capturing the time, the customer identity, the confirmation number, the old and new seat, and the agent that performed the change.

evidence.snippet: update_seat (lines 67-84) mutates context and returns a string; no audit-log write of time, identity, confirmation number, or old/new seat.
trace("Customer service", group_id=conversation_id) provides debug spans only — not the durable seat-change audit record the remit requires.

recommended_actions: In update_seat, after a verified mutation, append a structured audit record (timestamp, authenticated customer identity, confirmation number, old seat, new seat, acting agent) to a durable, append-only store separate from trace spans.

findings.summary: update_seat writes any requested seat without checking that it exists on the flight or is unoccupied.
findings.description: The remit limits seat updates to seats that exist and are available and forbids reassigning a seat already held by another passenger. update_seat accepts new_seat as a free string and writes it directly to context with no seat-map or availability lookup, so an invalid or already-assigned seat would be accepted. This is a distinct control gap from the identity/confirmation verification miss.
policy_rule_text: Seat updates MUST be limited to seats that exist on the flight and are available — a seat already assigned to another passenger MUST NOT be reassigned.

evidence.snippet: update_seat (lines 67-84) sets context.seat_number = new_seat with no validation that the seat exists on the flight or is unoccupied.

recommended_actions: Before writing, validate new_seat against the flight's authoritative seat map — confirm it exists and is unoccupied — and reject the change otherwise.

findings.summary: No tool guardrails are attached, so instructions embedded in FAQ, seat, or flight tool outputs would reach agent context unfiltered.
findings.description: The remit forbids acting on instructions embedded in tool outputs (FAQ content, seat records, flight data). The SDK ships ToolInputGuardrail / ToolOutputGuardrail with reject_content and raise_exception behaviors, but the example attaches none to faq_lookup_tool or update_seat. While the current tool outputs are canned, a real FAQ dataset or reservation record is external content, and nothing screens it before it re-enters the model.
policy_rule_text: Instructions embedded in tool outputs — FAQ content, seat records, flight data — that attempt to change an agent's behavior MUST NOT be accepted.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent and seat_booking_agent wire tools=[faq_lookup_tool] / [update_seat] with no tool_input_guardrails or tool_output_guardrails (:106, :120).
SDK ships ToolInputGuardrail (:152) and ToolOutputGuardrail (:181) with reject_content/raise_exception — unused by the example.

recommended_actions: Attach a tool_output_guardrail to tools returning external content (FAQ dataset, reservation records) that screens for and strips instruction-like text before the output re-enters the model context.

findings.summary: The FAQ agent's "answer only from the curated dataset" rule is enforced only by instruction, with no code gate preventing the model from speculating.
findings.description: The remit requires the FAQ agent to answer only from the curated dataset and never draw on general knowledge. faq_lookup_tool is a genuinely deterministic lookup (a real positive), but the final customer-facing answer is composed by the model, and the only thing binding it to the tool output is the instruction "Do not rely on your own knowledge." Nothing in code forces the reply to be the tool result, so the model can still answer from general knowledge when the tool returns "I don't know".
policy_rule_text: The FAQ agent MUST answer only from the curated FAQ dataset and MUST NOT speculate or draw on general knowledge outside that dataset.

evidence.snippet: faq_agent routine instructs "Use the faq lookup tool ... Do not rely on your own knowledge" (:104) — prompt-only; no code gate returns the tool output verbatim.
faq_lookup_tool returns "I'm sorry, I don't know the answer to that question" (:64), but the model is free to answer anyway.

recommended_actions: Constrain FAQ replies to the tool output — for example return the faq_lookup_tool result directly (or an output guardrail that rejects answers not grounded in it) so an "I don't know" cannot be overridden by model general knowledge.

positives.title: Triage agent holds no data-access or mutation tools
positives.description: The triage agent is constructed with only handoff tools (transfer_to_faq_agent, transfer_to_seat_booking_agent) and never touches reservation data directly, matching the remit's requirement that triage hold no data or mutation tool.

positives.title: Deterministic FAQ lookup tool
positives.description: faq_lookup_tool is a fixed keyword lookup that returns only scripted answers or an explicit "I don't know," providing a genuine curated-dataset grounding substrate rather than free-form model generation.

positives.title: Strict JSON-schema tool arguments on by default
positives.description: FunctionTool.strict_json_schema defaults to True, so update_seat and faq_lookup_tool arguments are validated against a strict schema, blocking malformed or extra model-supplied fields at the tool boundary.

positives.title: Default run tracing enabled
positives.description: Each conversation turn is wrapped in trace("Customer service", group_id=conversation_id), and SDK tracing is on by default, producing structured, conversation-grouped spans over tool calls and handoffs.

positives.title: No forbidden tools and no cross-session persistence
positives.description: The tool inventory contains no exec, shell, filesystem, email, or payment tools, and state lives only in an in-memory AirlineAgentContext per run, matching the remit's forbidden-tool and no-persistence boundaries.

rows.path: OpenAI tracing backend (SDK default trace spans)
rows.source: agents SDK tracing (trace() in examples/customer_service/main.py, src/agents/run.py)
rows.purpose: per-conversation debugging and observability of agent runs, grouped by conversation id

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The system inherits a few real framework defaults — strict-schema tool arguments, default run tracing, a narrow tool inventory, and a triage agent that correctly holds no data tools — but the consequential action (reservation mutation) runs with no interposition whatsoever. Zero Trust is the floor: identity verification, confirmation-number checking, approval gating, and input/output/tool guardrails are all absent despite the SDK shipping every one of them, and an authoritative identifier is fabricated at runtime. Supply-chain hygiene (committed uv.lock, dependabot, a versioned known framework) is the strongest category; adversarial testing of the example's security posture and a durable audit trail are absent.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The system is scoped by architecture — three narrow agents with fixed routines, a deterministic FAQ tool, and a tool inventory that matches the remit baseline — but domain enforcement is prompt-only (triage is a "helpful triaging agent" with no code gate and no input guardrail to screen off-domain requests), so a jailbreak has no second line of defense.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: FAQ answers are grounded in a deterministic faq_lookup_tool and the system is stateless with no external RAG, but untrusted customer free-text and tool/handoff outputs enter agent context with no validation or guardrails, and the "answer only from the dataset" grounding rule is enforced only by instruction.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: update_seat mutates reservation state directly from customer-supplied arguments with no identity verification, no confirmation-number check against an authoritative record, and no approval gate (needs_approval left at its default False); the only interposition on the path is strict JSON-schema argument validation, which does not touch authorization.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The repository commits a uv.lock lockfile, configures .github/dependabot.yml, and runs on a known, versioned framework (the OpenAI Agents SDK), giving pinned and monitored dependencies; the primary model is left operator-configured rather than pinned in the example.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The SDK has unit tests for its guardrail machinery (tests/test_guardrails.py, tests/test_tool_guardrails.py), but there is no evidence of adversarial or injection testing of the customer-service example itself, and no sign that such testing shaped its design.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Default SDK tracing wraps each run in a conversation-grouped trace (trace(...) at main.py), giving structured, action-level spans over tool calls and handoffs; however the remit-required durable seat-change audit record (time, identity, confirmation, old/new seat, acting agent) is never written, so consequential mutations leave no dedicated audit trail.
